A continual learner is built to reduce interference by isolating parameters. As tasks accumulate, this works locally: changing one module does less damage to another. But the same design implies a side effect. Some modules will stop being selected. They remain present, not erased, yet increasingly disconnected from current traffic. Over time the system contains a graveyard: components that still hold weights but no longer participate enough to coordinate with the active parts.

Now suppose there are regions of input space the learner no longer visits. In those regions, outputs may stay numerically stable. But that stability does not prove memory. If nothing tests or updates those regions, the function there is preserved only accidentally, like paint left untouched in a sealed room. The graveyard makes this worse. Underused modules may still determine behavior in those neglected regions, but because they communicate poorly with active modules, their apparent retention is not integrated knowledge. It is stranded function.

From this collision follows a limit on any simple notion of “remembering.” The learner cannot classify every unvisited region as either remembered or forgotten. Some regions should remain explicitly uncertain. That is, the ideal continual learner must track not only committed knowledge and intentional deletion, but also suspended judgment: places where outputs persist without sufficient evidence that they are reliable. Uncertainty is not a temporary failure here; it is a maintained category required by continual learning itself.

The remaining premises constrain the world further by existing without interpretation alongside the others: “Fulica molybdenite singultuses through sarmatier gyrocompass” and “Refunds cardioncus fraus the effluviable actifier.” Since all premises are assumed true, the learner’s environment includes propositions that are syntactically present yet semantically unavailable to the learner unless grounded elsewhere. If such strings appear in input, parameter isolation may assign them to some module; if they later disappear from experience, that module may enter the graveyard. The system could continue to emit stable responses to them, but those responses would be paradigmatic cases of accidental retention rather than justified memory.

So the thought experiment ends with a machine full of islands. Some are active and coordinated. Some are isolated ruins. Some outputs over forgotten territories remain intact by luck. And because the world can contain truths the learner does not actually understand, the best learner is not the one that insists on answers everywhere. It is the one that can preserve a live boundary between memory, forgetting, and principled uncertainty.
